title: Phase 1: Walking Skeleton — First-Release Cut, End-to-End
Project skeleton & CommandRunner seam
description:
Problem: Greenfield Go project (no go.mod, no source). Every later task invokes git/gh/claude as external processes; the fragile logic around those is why the spec chose Go. No execution seam exists yet, so nothing can be built or tested in isolation from the real binaries.

Solution: Initialise the Go module and package layout, then define a single CommandRunner interface abstracting external-command execution, with a real os/exec-backed implementation and a fake for tests. Every subsequent git/gh/claude call goes through this seam.

Outcome: go build ./... and go test ./... succeed on a clean checkout; a CommandRunner runs a command returning stdout, stderr, and exit status; the fake lets a test script any named command's result (incl. non-zero exits and command-not-found) without touching the host.

Do:
- go mod init github.com/leeovery/mint at repo root (Go 1.22+).
- Package internal/exec (or internal/runner) with CommandRunner interface: Result{Stdout, Stderr string; ExitCode int}; Run(ctx, name, args...) (Result, error); plus a stdin affordance (RunWith(ctx, stdin io.Reader, ...) or options) because Stage 4 pipes a prompt to 'claude -p' on stdin — establish the stdin path now though Phase 1 doesn't exercise AI.
- ExecRunner via os/exec.CommandContext: capture stdout/stderr separately; populate ExitCode from process exit (*exec.ExitError); distinct sentinel/typed error for binary-not-found (exec.ErrNotFound / errors.Is).
- FakeRunner: matches on command name (optionally args), returns pre-seeded Result/error, records invocation order, supports seeding command-not-found.
- Convention: non-zero exit returns a populated Result WITH a non-nil error so callers can inspect Stderr/ExitCode and branch on err.

Acceptance Criteria:
- go build ./... and go test ./... pass on clean checkout, no external binaries.
- ExecRunner.Run returns stdout/stderr separately + correct ExitCode for success and failure.
- Non-zero exit surfaced (non-nil error) with Stderr/ExitCode still readable on Result.
- Missing binary surfaced as distinguishable command-not-found, not generic failure.
- FakeRunner scripts results, records invocation order, simulates command-not-found, zero host interaction.

Tests: runs command returns stdout; captures stderr separately; surfaces non-zero exit+stderr; command-not-found distinct from non-zero exit; FakeRunner seeded results + invocation order; FakeRunner simulates command-not-found.

Edge Cases: non-zero exit captured with stderr (preflight/abort messages depend on it); command-not-found surfaced distinctly (the gh gate in 1-8 relies on telling missing apart from ran-and-failed).

Context: Settled foundation — Go chosen for testability of git/gh/claude invocation behind a single CommandRunner interface that can mock those commands. Runtime tools invoked behind the CommandRunner seam are runtime prerequisites, not build/spec deps. Stage 4 pipes the prompt to stdin and reads stdout — hence the stdin affordance now.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Settled foundations; Dependencies/Notes.

title: Minimal config load (tag_prefix, commit_prefix, release_branch, publish)
description:
Problem: The release pipeline needs a handful of [release] config values (tag_prefix, commit_prefix, release_branch, publish) to compute tags, write commit/tag subjects, gate the branch check, and decide whether to publish. Full schema and fail-loud validation are Phase 6, but Phase 1 still needs these four values loaded with correct defaults so the zero-config skeleton runs.

Solution: A minimal TOML config loader that reads .mint.toml from the repo root, extracts only the four Phase 1 keys from the [release] table, applies defaults when the file or any key is absent. No strict unknown-key/bad-type validation yet (Phase 6) — unknown keys tolerated/ignored.

Solution note: deliberately narrow slice. Do NOT implement the full schema, shared engine keys, [release.hooks], or fail-loud validation here.

Outcome: No .mint.toml -> tag_prefix=v, commit_prefix=emoji, release_branch="" (auto-derive, resolved in 1-4), publish=true. Partial file -> present keys override, absent keys default. Comments-only/blank file behaves as absent.

Do:
- Add a TOML decoder dep (github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2 or github.com/BurntSushi/toml; pick one, go get).
- Package internal/config with a Config struct (or Release sub-struct): TagPrefix, CommitPrefix, ReleaseBranch string; Publish bool.
- Load(root string) (Config, error): read {root}/.mint.toml; if absent return defaults; decode [release] table.
- Defaults: tag_prefix->v, commit_prefix->emoji, release_branch->"" (sentinel for auto-derive), publish->true. Because publish's zero value is false, distinguish absent from explicit-false (decode into *bool then default nil->true, or pre-seed struct before decode).
- Explicit tag_prefix="" is a valid value (prefix-less tags), must NOT coerce back to v; only an ABSENT key defaults.
- Do not error on unknown keys (Phase 6 adds fail-loud); malformed-TOML may surface as an error.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Absent .mint.toml -> all four defaults (v, emoji, "", true).
- Subset of keys -> overrides those, rest at default.
- publish=false honoured (not lost to bool zero-value ambiguity).
- Explicit tag_prefix="" preserved as empty, not re-defaulted.
- Blank/comments-only file behaves exactly like absent.
- Loader testable standalone against a temp dir.

Tests: returns all defaults when absent; overrides only present keys; honours publish=false; preserves explicit empty tag_prefix; treats blank/comments file as defaults; returns configured commit_prefix and release_branch.

Edge Cases: file absent->defaults; subset present->rest default; comments/blank->defaults; publish=false vs absent (bool trap); explicit empty tag_prefix.

Context: Config fully optional, zero config = sensible defaults everywhere. Schema defaults: tag_prefix=v, commit_prefix=emoji (cosmetic), release_branch auto-derived from origin/HEAD, publish=true (false = tag+push only). Typed fail-loud validation is consolidated in Phase 6; Phase 1 loads only this subset. TOML at .mint.toml at repo root, resolved via git rev-parse --show-toplevel.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Config Format & Schema.

title: Version determination from git tags
description:
Problem: Version is sourced entirely from git tags — no file/embedded source. mint must read the complete tag set, parse only strict 3-part SemVer tags matching the configured prefix, find the global numeric maximum (not git describe's nearest-reachable), and treat no-matching-tags as 0.0.0. Without this no version can be computed and the first-release path can't start.

Solution: A version package that lists tags via CommandRunner, parses each against ^{tag_prefix}(\d+)\.(\d+)\.(\d+)$, ignores non-matching, returns the highest matching SemVer (or 0.0.0), plus a bump function computing the next version.

Outcome: CurrentVersion returns the global max matching SemVer or 0.0.0; Next(current, bump) bumps patch/minor/major. No tags: default(patch)->0.0.1, minor->0.1.0, major->1.0.0. Non-conforming tags ignored.

Do:
- Package internal/version with SemVer{Major,Minor,Patch int} and a Bump kind (patch/minor/major).
- List tags through CommandRunner (git tag --list or git for-each-ref --format '%(refname:short)' refs/tags). The fetch --tags is a preflight concern (1-6); this task reads what's present.
- Parse against exact pattern ^{tag_prefix}(\d+)\.(\d+)\.(\d+)$ (regexp.QuoteMeta the prefix). Skip non-matching: 1.2, 1.2.0-rc.1, 1.2.0.4, 1.2.0+build5, release-1.2, different/absent prefix.
- CurrentVersion(prefix): parse all matching, return numeric max by (Major,Minor,Patch); none -> 0.0.0.
- Next(current, bump): patch->Patch+1; minor->Minor+1,Patch=0; major->Major+1,Minor=0,Patch=0.
- String(prefix)/tag-format helper writing the prefix back: {prefix}{M}.{m}.{p} (used by tagging 1-10 and free-tag checks 1-5/1-6).
- Numeric (not lexical) comparison: v10.0.0>v9.0.0, v1.10.0>v1.9.0.

Acceptance Criteria:
- No matching tags -> 0.0.0.
- No tags: default->0.0.1, minor->0.1.0, major->1.0.0.
- Non-conforming tags (1.2, 1.2.0-rc.1, 1.2.0.4, 1.2.0+build5, release-1.2) ignored.
- Non-matching prefix ignored; only {tag_prefix}... count; empty prefix matches bare X.Y.Z.
- Latest = global numeric maximum, double-digit segments numeric (v10.0.0>v9.0.0).
- Tag formatting writes the configured prefix back (v0.0.1, or 0.0.1 with empty prefix).

Tests: returns 0.0.0 when no tags; 0.0.0 when none match prefix; ignores non-3-part-semver (rc/4-seg/build/release-1.2); global numeric max not lexical; ignores other prefixes, matches configured; matches bare X.Y.Z when prefix empty; bumps patch/minor/major incl from 0.0.0; formats version back with prefix.

Edge Cases: no matching tags->0.0.0; non-matching ignored (1.2, 1.2.0-rc.1, 1.2.0.4, release-1.2); mixed prefixes (only configured counts); double-digit segments numeric not lexical.

Context: Current version is highest SemVer tag (prefix-stripped); no file/embedded source; none->0.0.0. Latest = numerically highest matching globally, not git describe. Strict SemVer 2.0.0, 3 numeric segments only; anything else ignored. Pattern ^{tag_prefix}(\d+)\.(\d+)\.(\d+)$. tag_prefix default v, overridable to "". First release: no tags->0.0.0 so release->0.0.1, -m->0.1.0, -M->1.0.0. --set-version and explicit-version validation deferred to Phase 4 — do not implement here.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 1 Version Determination & Tag Grammar.

title: Repo root anchoring & release-branch resolution
description:
Problem: mint must anchor every operation at the repo root and know the release branch before running preflight gates. Root via git rev-parse --show-toplevel; release branch defaults to one auto-derived from origin/HEAD (resolves main/master with zero config) unless overridden by config. Without these, config loading (reads .mint.toml at root) and the branch gate (1-5) have no anchor.

Solution: A repository-context resolver using CommandRunner to find the repo root (failing cleanly if not a git repo) and determine the release branch — config override if set, else the branch origin/HEAD points at.

Outcome: ResolveRoot returns absolute repo root or a clean 'not a git repository' abort. ResolveReleaseBranch returns configured release_branch if non-empty, else the branch derived from origin/HEAD (e.g. main), surfacing a clear condition when origin/HEAD unset and no override.

Do:
- Package internal/gitrepo (or internal/repo): ResolveRoot(runner) runs git rev-parse --show-toplevel; success->trimmed path; failure->clean error suitable for an abort message, not a panic.
- Code comment recording spec resolution semantics: --show-toplevel resolves to innermost enclosing repo or linked worktree; submodule is its own repo, linked worktree shares main repo's ref store. No special handling required — git is authoritative — comment records intent.
- ResolveReleaseBranch(runner, cfg): if cfg.ReleaseBranch != "" return it; else derive from origin/HEAD (git symbolic-ref --short refs/remotes/origin/HEAD -> origin/main, strip origin/; or git rev-parse --abbrev-ref origin/HEAD). Return short branch name (main/master).
- origin/HEAD unset: if derivation fails and no override, return a distinguishable error/condition. Phase 1 may surface as abort; do NOT auto-pick main silently.
- Wire the config-loaded release_branch (1-2) as override source.

Acceptance Criteria:
- ResolveRoot returns repo root via git rev-parse --show-toplevel.
- Outside a git repo, ResolveRoot aborts cleanly (no panic).
- ResolveReleaseBranch returns config release_branch when set, ignoring origin/HEAD.
- No override -> derives from origin/HEAD (e.g. main).
- origin/HEAD unset and no override -> clear distinguishable condition, no silent default.
- All git calls via CommandRunner, exercised with FakeRunner.

Tests: resolves repo root; aborts cleanly when not a git repo; returns configured release_branch when set; derives from origin/HEAD when no override; surfaces clear condition when origin/HEAD unset and no override.

Edge Cases: not a git repo->clean abort; origin/HEAD unset->distinguishable condition (no silent default); release_branch override->used verbatim, derivation skipped.

Context: Gate 1 — git repo present, anchored at repo root via git rev-parse --show-toplevel. Submodule/worktree resolution: innermost enclosing repo or linked worktree. Gate 2 — on the release branch, default-on, auto-derived from origin/HEAD, override via release_branch. Config location resolved via show-toplevel. --any-branch escape hatch deferred to Phase 4.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 2 Preflight & Safety Gates (gates 1-2); Config Format & Schema/Location.

title: Local preflight gates (clean tree, on branch, tag-free local)
description:
Problem: Releasing is high-consequence, so mint forces a known-good starting state before any mutation. The cheap local gates — clean working tree, on the release branch, target tag not already existing locally — must run (cheap-first order) and abort cleanly. Without them a re-run could double-release or a dirty tree could be tagged.

Solution: A preflight package implementing the three local gates over CommandRunner, each returning a clear pass/fail with an actionable abort message, runnable in order before any network call or mutation.

Outcome: Clean tree, current branch == release branch, no local tag matching computed {tag_prefix}X.Y.Z -> all pass. Dirty tree / off-branch / pre-existing local tag each abort cleanly with a specific message. Gitignored files never trip clean-tree.

Do:
- internal/preflight CheckCleanTree(runner): git status --porcelain; non-empty -> fail. Gitignored exempt by default (porcelain excludes them); block on uncommitted/unstaged tracked changes AND non-ignored untracked files. Do NOT pass --ignored.
- CheckOnBranch(runner, releaseBranch): current branch (git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD or git symbolic-ref --short HEAD); fail if differs from releaseBranch (1-4), message naming both.
- CheckTagFreeLocal(runner, tag): check {tag} exists locally (git rev-parse -q --verify refs/tags/{tag} or git tag --list {tag}); exists -> fail (tag {tag} already exists).
- Order local gates cheap-first: clean tree, on branch, tag-free local. Network gates (remote sync, tag-free remote) are 1-6, run after.
- Each gate returns a typed failure carrying a human abort message; nothing mutates.
- --autostash (clean-tree bypass) and --any-branch (branch bypass) are Phase 4 — do NOT implement here.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Clean + on branch + tag-free local -> all pass.
- Dirty tracked changes -> clean-tree fails.
- Non-ignored untracked files -> clean-tree fails.
- Gitignored files only -> clean-tree passes (exempt).
- Branch != release branch -> on-branch fails naming both.
- Local tag matching computed tag exists -> tag-free-local fails.
- Gates cheap-first (tree,branch,tag), abort on first failure; all via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: passes when clean/on-branch/tag-free; fails on uncommitted tracked changes; fails on non-ignored untracked files; passes when only gitignored present; fails when not on release branch; fails when target tag exists locally.

Edge Cases: dirty tracked changes->fail; non-ignored untracked->fail; gitignored exempt->pass; not on release branch->fail; tag exists locally->fail.

Context: Gate 3 — clean working tree strict, git status --porcelain must be empty, gitignored exempt, blocks on uncommitted/unstaged tracked AND non-ignored untracked. Gate 2 — on the release branch. Gate 4 — target tag free, computed {tag_prefix}X.Y.Z must not exist locally or remote; closes the double-release/re-run footgun. Checks run cheap local first then network. --autostash/--any-branch deferred to Phase 4.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 2 Preflight & Safety Gates (gates 2-4).

title: Network preflight gates (fetch --tags, remote sync, tag-free remote)
description:
Problem: Local gates can't see the remote. mint must fetch (incl. --tags, always seeing the complete tag set) then refuse to release if local branch is behind or diverged from upstream (auto-pulling would silently drag in and release unseen commits), and refuse if target tag exists on remote. Being ahead is fine — those are the commits being released.

Solution: The network half of preflight: a fetch that pulls tags, a remote-sync gate computing ahead/behind against the release branch's upstream, and a remote tag-free check — all over CommandRunner, read-only, aborting cleanly, never auto-pulling.

Outcome: After git fetch --tags, up-to-date or ahead passes; behind aborts with a commit count ('N commits behind origin/main — pull and review, then re-run'); diverged aborts. Target tag on remote aborts. mint never pulls.

Do:
- internal/preflight Fetch(runner): git fetch --tags (and the release branch upstream) so full tag set and upstream refs are current. Must run before version 'latest' is trusted and before remote gates. Coordinate ordering with 1-11 (fetch precedes remote-sync and remote-tag checks).
- CheckRemoteSync(runner, releaseBranch): compute ahead/behind vs upstream — git rev-list --left-right --count {upstream}...HEAD (or @{u}...HEAD). Behind (>0 behind) or diverged (>0 behind AND >0 ahead) -> fail with clear message incl behind count; up-to-date or purely ahead -> pass. Never git pull.
- No upstream configured: surface a clear condition (no tracking remote) rather than crash; distinguishable preflight result.
- CheckTagFreeRemote(runner, tag): after fetch the tag appears locally if it exists remotely; alternatively probe git ls-remote --tags origin refs/tags/{tag}. Non-empty -> fail (tag {tag} already exists on remote).
- Keep all read-only; no mutation.

Acceptance Criteria:
- git fetch --tags runs before remote gates so full tag set visible.
- Behind upstream -> abort with behind commit count in message.
- Diverged (both ahead and behind) -> abort.
- Ahead only (or up-to-date) -> pass.
- mint never runs git pull.
- No upstream -> distinguishable clearly-reported condition (no crash).
- Target tag on remote -> abort.

Tests: fetches with --tags before remote checks; aborts with count when behind; aborts when diverged; passes when ahead; passes when up-to-date; never invokes git pull; reports clearly when no upstream; aborts when target tag exists on remote.

Edge Cases: behind->abort with count; diverged->abort; ahead->pass; no upstream->clear distinguishable condition; tag exists on remote->abort.

Context: Gate 5 — remote sync, git fetch then abort (never auto-pull) if behind or diverged from the release branch's upstream; being ahead is fine and expected; auto-pulling would silently drag in unseen remote commits; integrating remote work must be a conscious act; clear abort message e.g. 'N commits behind origin/main — pull and review, then re-run'. Gate 4 — target tag free, must not exist locally or remote. Preflight fetch includes --tags so mint always sees the complete tag set even after a fresh/partial clone. The gh install+auth gate is task 1-8 (only when publishing, before the tag).

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 2 Preflight & Safety Gates (gates 4-5); Stage 1 Source of truth.

title: Presenter interface & recording fake
description:
Problem: The release run emits semantic events (plan summary, computed version, gate results, notes, review gate, push/publish outcomes) to the user, but gate RENDERING and concrete pretty/plain renderers are owned by the separate CLI Presentation specification (a cross-spec dependency not built here). The engine must still compile and be fully testable without a concrete renderer.

Solution: Define the Presenter interface — the event/method surface the engine calls — and a recording/fake implementation for tests that captures every emitted event. The engine is wired to emit through this interface only; no concrete renderer is required to build or test it.

Outcome: A Presenter interface exists with the semantic methods the Phase 1 pipeline needs; a RecordingPresenter captures emitted events so tests assert what the engine reported. The engine builds and all Phase 1 tests pass against the fake, no concrete renderer present.

Do:
- internal/presenter: define a Presenter interface covering the semantic events the Phase 1 forward path emits. Keep methods semantic (what happened) not presentational (how to draw). Suggested surface (confirm against pipeline needs):
  - plan/version summary (Plan(summary PlanInfo) carrying current->next version, tag, publish target).
  - stage/gate progress and failure (GateFailed(name,message) or generic Step/Warn/Error).
  - notes shown (the body about to be recorded).
  - push and publish outcomes incl. post-PONR warn-only (Warn(message) used by 1-11's publish-failure path).
- The interactive notes-review gate's RENDERING and the four-choice mapping belong to the CLI Presentation spec; model the engine's need as a minimal decision request (Confirm/gate method returning a choice) so the engine has a seam — do NOT implement gate rendering. Phase 1 first-release gate is y/n/e only (no r); -y bypasses. The fake returns a scripted choice.
- RecordingPresenter: records each call (method+payload) in order; for any decision method returns a pre-seeded answer; assertion helpers (last warning, recorded events).
- Doc comment marking this interface as the cross-spec boundary: concrete pretty/plain renderers and gate rendering owned by the CLI Presentation specification; the engine depends only on this interface.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Presenter interface with semantic methods for Phase 1 (plan/version, gate failure, notes shown, push/publish outcome incl warn).
- A decision/gate seam exists so the engine requests the review choice without owning rendering.
- RecordingPresenter captures events in order and returns scripted decisions.
- Engine builds and Phase 1 tests run entirely against the fake — no concrete renderer referenced.
- A doc comment records the cross-spec ownership boundary.

Tests: recording presenter captures emitted events in order; returns a scripted review-gate choice; records a warn (post-PONR) event; engine compiles and reports through Presenter with no concrete renderer.

Edge Cases: none.

Context: Dependencies/Partial Requirement — the release run emits semantic events to a Presenter and shows the interactive notes-review gate through it; this spec defers gate rendering and --plain behaviour to that spec. Minimum scope: the Presenter interface (event/method surface) and the notes-review gate rendering contract; concrete pretty/plain renderers and --plain detection land in parallel — engine builds and tests against the interface (a fake/recording presenter). Interactive review Phase 1 first-release: the gate offers only y/n/e on no-AI paths (r omitted, no AI to nudge); -y skips. Phase 1 builds the seam; full four-choice gate behaviour lands in Phase 2.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Dependencies/Partial Requirement; Interactive Confirmation & Notes Review.

title: Publisher interface & GitHub driver (gh gate when publishing)
description:
Problem: Publishing the GitHub release is first-class and provider-abstracted — not a hook, not hardcoded to gh at the call site. mint must gate gh install+auth ONLY when actually publishing and BEFORE the tag, so a missing/unauthenticated gh never strands a pushed tag with no release. Phase 1 needs the Publisher seam plus the GitHub driver and that conditional gate.

Solution: A Publisher interface (CreateRelease/UpdateRelease) with a GitHub driver that shells gh through CommandRunner, plus a gh install+auth preflight check that runs conditionally (only when publish=true) and is sequenced before tag creation.

Outcome: When publish=true, mint verifies gh installed+authenticated before the tag; if missing/unauthenticated it aborts before any tag/push. When publish=false, the gh gate is skipped and no publish attempted. The GitHub driver's CreateRelease invokes gh release create ... --verify-tag with the tag and notes body via CommandRunner.

Do:
- internal/publish: type Publisher interface { CreateRelease(ctx, tag, title, body string) error; UpdateRelease(ctx, tag, title, body string) error }. Phase 1 exercises CreateRelease; UpdateRelease is part of the seam for Phase 5 — stub it but keep on the interface.
- GitHubPublisher backed by CommandRunner: CreateRelease runs gh release create {tag} --title {title} --notes {body} --verify-tag (pass body safely, e.g. --notes-file - on stdin or a temp file, to avoid arg-length/escaping issues with a long body).
- CheckGhAuth(runner) in preflight: verify gh installed (command-not-found -> fail 'gh not installed') and authenticated (gh auth status non-zero -> fail 'gh not authenticated'). Distinguish the two using the command-not-found signal from 1-1.
- Make the gate conditional and ordered: caller (1-11) runs CheckGhAuth ONLY when publish=true and AFTER other preflight gates but BEFORE tag creation. Document this ordering invariant.
- Phase 1 provider is GitHub directly. Full auto-detection from remote host and unknown-provider/no-driver downgrade are Phase 4 — do NOT implement detection/downgrade here; assume GitHub when publish=true.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Publisher interface with CreateRelease and UpdateRelease; GitHubPublisher implements it over CommandRunner.
- CreateRelease invokes gh release create {tag} ... --verify-tag with title and full notes body passed safely.
- When publish=true, the gh gate runs before tag creation.
- gh missing -> abort before any tag/push, 'not installed' message.
- gh unauthenticated -> abort before any tag/push, 'not authenticated' message.
- When publish=false, gh gate skipped and no publish attempted.
- All gh invocations via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: CreateRelease invokes gh release create with tag/title/body and --verify-tag; gh gate passes when installed+authenticated; aborts before tag when gh not installed; aborts before tag when gh not authenticated; gh gate skipped when publish=false; no publish attempted when publish=false.

Edge Cases: publish=false->no gh gate/publish; gh missing->abort before tag; gh unauthenticated->abort before tag.

Context: Gate 6 — gh installed+authenticated, gated only when actually publishing a GitHub release, and before the tag, so a missing/unauthenticated gh never strands a pushed tag with no release. Publishing first-class but provider-abstracted — not hardcoded to gh, not a post_release hook. Behind a small Publisher interface (CreateRelease/UpdateRelease). mint auto-detects provider from remote host (github.com -> GitHub via gh), overridable by provider config. GitHub is the only driver now. publish default true, false = tag+push only. Auto-detection from host and the unknown-provider/no-driver loud-downgrade are Phase 4 — not here.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 2 gate 6; Stages 6-7 Publishing provider driver abstraction.

title: First-release body & Record (changelog + bookkeeping commit)
description:
Problem: On a first release there is no prior tag to diff and diffing the whole repo is useless, so mint must skip the AI entirely and use the fixed body 'Initial release.'. That single body flows into Record: mint writes the CHANGELOG entry (creating the file with a Keep a Changelog preamble if absent) and creates the one release-bookkeeping commit. Phase 1 has no hooks and no version-file projection, so this is a single bookkeeping commit.

Solution: A first-release body provider returning the fixed 'Initial release.' string (no AI invocation), plus the Record stage: a Keep a Changelog writer that prepends a ## [x.y.z] - YYYY-MM-DD section (creating the file with the standard preamble when absent) and stages+commits it as {commit_prefix} Release {tag}.

Outcome: For a no-prior-tag run, notes body is exactly 'Initial release.' with no claude invocation recorded. The CHANGELOG gets a dated first version section (file created with KaC preamble if absent), and mint produces one commit subjected {commit_prefix} Release {tag}. If the changelog write yields no net change, no commit is made.

Do:
- First-release body provider returns constant 'Initial release.' and never calls AI transport. Highest-precedence notes path; for Phase 1 it's the only path (AI engine arrives Phase 2).
- internal/record (or internal/changelog) changelog writer:
  - Absent CHANGELOG.md -> create with standard Keep a Changelog header preamble, then the first version section.
  - Insert new ## [{x.y.z}] - {YYYY-MM-DD} section (full body) below KaC preamble and above any prior ## [ block (newest on top). First release has no prior sections.
  - Use current date for the header.
  - Skip changelog entirely when changelog=false (config). Phase 1 minimal config doesn't expose changelog; treat absent as default true. The changelog-key wiring can be deferred, but honour the no-op-skip behaviour.
- Release-bookkeeping commit: stage the changelog change, commit with subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} (e.g. emoji Release v0.0.1) via CommandRunner (git add + git commit).
- No-op safety: if changelog write produces no net change (nothing to stage), skip the commit (no empty commit).
- Phase 1 scope: exactly one (bookkeeping) commit. Optional hook-artifact commit and version-file projection fold-in are Phase 3 — do NOT implement here.

Acceptance Criteria:
- First-release body is exactly 'Initial release.' with no AI/claude invocation.
- Absent CHANGELOG.md -> created with KaC preamble plus first ## [x.y.z] - date section.
- Existing CHANGELOG.md -> new section inserted below preamble, newest on top (no prior section for first release).
- Section header uses ## [x.y.z] - YYYY-MM-DD with current date.
- Release-bookkeeping commit subject is {commit_prefix} Release {tag} (default emoji Release v0.0.1).
- No-op changelog -> no commit created.

Tests: first-release body is 'Initial release.' with no AI call; creates CHANGELOG.md with KaC preamble when absent; inserts first version section with current date; prepends new section below preamble newest on top; commits with subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag}; skips commit when changelog write is a no-op.

Edge Cases: CHANGELOG.md absent -> create with KaC preamble; no-op changelog -> skip commit (no empty commit).

Context: Diff base — first release (no prior tag): no base to diff, diffing whole repo useless -> mint skips AI and uses fixed body 'Initial release.'. Notes-path precedence (1): first release wins over everything below. Changelog mechanics: complete ## [x.y.z] - YYYY-MM-DD section (full notes body) written atomically, inserted above the most recent existing ## [ block; first release creates the file with KaC preamble then first section; newest on top; no [Unreleased]. Commit graph: release bookkeeping — CHANGELOG entry and version-file projection folded into one commit {commit_prefix} Release {tag}. No-op safety: no empty commits. Hook-artifact commit and version-file projection are Phase 3.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Diff base; Notes-path precedence; Stage 5 Record.

title: Annotated tag & atomic push
description:
Problem: Stage 6 is the single point of no return. mint must create an annotated tag (signable, offline, immutable) whose subject is {commit_prefix} Release {tag} and whose body is the full notes, then git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag} so commit(s) and tag go up together or not at all. A rejected push must be surfaced and must not lead to a publish attempt.

Solution: A tag+push module: create the annotated tag at the release-bookkeeping commit with subject+body, then run the atomic push, treating push success as crossing the point of no return and a push failure as a clean pre-PONR abort that surfaces and stops before publishing.

Outcome: mint creates an annotated tag {tag} pointing at the bookkeeping commit, subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag}, body = full notes ('Initial release.' in Phase 1). git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag} sends commit(s)+tag together. A rejected push is surfaced via the Presenter and no publish is attempted.

Do:
- internal/release (tag/push area): annotated-tag creation via CommandRunner: git tag -a {tag} -F - (body on stdin) or -m with subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} followed by the full notes body. Annotated (not lightweight) is mandatory — the annotation body is the single source mint ever reads later.
  - Compose tag message: subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} (e.g. emoji Release v0.0.1) + blank line + full body.
- Atomic push: git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag}. This is the point of no return — commits+tag together.
- On push rejection/failure (non-zero exit): surface (Presenter error + abort) and STOP — do not proceed to publish. Because this is still pre-PONR (push did not succeed), nothing was published; the surgical local auto-unwind that deletes the just-made tag and resets the commit(s) is Phase 4 (this task surfaces the failure; it does not implement the full unwind).
- On push success: signal to the caller that PONR has been crossed (publish may proceed; from here failures are warn-only — wired in 1-11).
- Lock-resilient git wrapping (retry/clear stale .git lock) is Phase 4 — call the runner directly here; do not implement lock resilience.

Acceptance Criteria:
- An annotated tag (not lightweight) created at the bookkeeping commit with subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag}.
- The tag annotation body carries the full notes body verbatim.
- git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag} is the push form (commits+tag together).
- A rejected/failed push is surfaced via the Presenter and the run stops.
- No publish attempted when push fails.
- Push success signals the PONR crossing so the caller may publish.
- All git operations via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: creates annotated tag with subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} and full body; pushes with git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag}; surfaces a rejected push and stops; does not attempt publish when push fails; signals point of no return on push success.

Edge Cases: push rejected -> surfaced; no publish attempted.

Context: Tag annotation — subject {commit_prefix} Release {tag} + the FULL notes body (default commit_prefix emoji). Annotated (not lightweight): signable, offline, in-repo, immutable. This is the single source mint ever reads. Point of no return — git push --atomic origin HEAD {tag} is the single PONR, commits+tag go up together or not at all. Failure model before the push: everything mint did is local-only, mint auto-unwinds its own mutations — the surgical auto-unwind itself is Phase 4; Phase 1 surfaces the failure and stops. Lock-resilient git wrapping is also Phase 4. Optionality stack: annotated tag mandatory, always created, always carries a body.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Body Distribution Tag annotation; Stages 6-7 Point of no return; Stage 5 Commit graph.

title: Release command wiring (end-to-end first-release)
description:
Problem: The Phase 1 pieces — config, version, preflight gates, Presenter, Publisher, first-release body/Record, tag/push — exist independently but nothing threads them into a runnable mint release that takes a no-tags repo to a published 0.0.1. This task is the vertical seam that proves the spine end-to-end and demonstrates the point-of-no-return asymmetry (warn-only after push).

Solution: The CLI entry point and release orchestrator: parse the bump flag and -y, run the pipeline in spine order (version -> preflight -> first-release body -> record -> gh gate -> tag -> atomic push -> publish), and apply the PONR asymmetry — pre-push failures abort, a publish failure after the push warns only.

Outcome: In a no-matching-tags repo, mint release produces 0.0.1 end-to-end (computed version, gates passed, 'Initial release.' recorded, annotated tag, git push --atomic, GitHub release created). -m -> 0.1.0, -M -> 1.0.0, default -> 0.0.1. A publish failure after a successful push warns only (tag already public); a pre-push failure aborts.

Do:
- cmd/mint/main.go (or a cmd package) wiring a CLI. Implement mint release [bump] with flags: -p/--patch (default), -m/--minor, -M/--major, -y/--yes. (--set-version, -d/--dry-run, --no-ai, --autostash, --any-branch are later phases — do not implement their behaviour now; may reserve mint version and the release subcommand surface but only mint release needs to work.)
- Orchestrator running the spine in order:
  1. Resolve repo root (1-4); load config (1-2).
  2. Resolve release branch (1-4); determine current version + compute next from the bump (1-3).
  3. Preflight: fetch --tags, then local gates (clean tree, on branch, tag-free local — 1-5) and network gates (remote sync, tag-free remote — 1-6). Order: cheap local first then network. Abort cleanly on any gate failure (surfaced via Presenter).
  4. Build notes body: first-release path -> 'Initial release.' (1-9), no AI.
  5. Show plan + notes via Presenter; honour the review gate unless -y (Phase 1 first-release gate is y/n/e; n aborts — for Phase 1 abort = surface and stop, full auto-unwind is Phase 4).
  6. Record: write changelog + bookkeeping commit (1-9).
  7. If publish=true: run the gh install+auth gate (1-8) — BEFORE the tag.
  8. Create the annotated tag and git push --atomic (1-10). Push success = PONR crossed.
  9. If publish=true: Publisher.CreateRelease with the tag, title, and body (1-8).
- PONR asymmetry: any failure in steps 1-8 before push succeeds -> abort (surface, stop). A CreateRelease failure in step 9 (after a successful push) -> warn only via Presenter ('tag is already published; heal with regenerate --reuse'), exit without unwinding.
- Wire the recording Presenter in tests to assert the full event sequence and the warn-only publish-failure case; wire the FakeRunner to script git/gh.

Acceptance Criteria:
- No-tags repo: mint release resolves 0.0.0 -> 0.0.1 and runs the full spine to a created GitHub release.
- -m -> 0.1.0; -M -> 1.0.0; no flag (default) -> 0.0.1.
- Pipeline runs in spine order: version -> preflight (local then network) -> first-release body -> record -> gh gate (if publishing, before tag) -> tag -> atomic push -> publish.
- A pre-push gate/step failure aborts cleanly; nothing tagged/pushed/published.
- A publish failure AFTER a successful push warns only (post-PONR), does not unwind.
- With publish=false, no gh gate and no publish; run ends at a successful tag + atomic push.
- Whole flow exercised with FakeRunner and RecordingPresenter; no real git/gh/claude in tests.

Tests: mint release on a no-tags repo produces 0.0.1 end-to-end and creates the GitHub release; -m produces 0.1.0 and -M produces 1.0.0; default bump produces 0.0.1; a failing preflight gate aborts before any mutation; a publish failure after a successful push warns only and does not unwind; publish=false ends at a successful atomic push with no gh gate or publish; the spine runs stages in order.

Edge Cases: -m -> 0.1.0, -M -> 1.0.0, default -> 0.0.1; publish failure after push -> warn only (post-PONR); publish=false -> tag+push only, no gh gate.

Context: Release Lifecycle spine (strict order): Version -> Preflight -> Project prep -> Release notes -> Record -> Make official (tag + git push --atomic) -> Publish. Invariants: everything before stage 6 is local-only and recoverable; git push --atomic (stage 6) is the single PONR; after PONR mint never unwinds; failures in stage 7 warn and point to the heal path. Failure model: push succeeds but provider release create fails — tag already public, mint never unwinds, warns and points to heal regenerate --reuse. First-release: release->0.0.1, -m->0.1.0, -M->1.0.0. Phase 1 surfaces failures and stops; full surgical auto-unwind, --autostash restore ordering, and lock resilience are Phase 4. --set-version/--dry-run/--no-ai/--any-branch are later phases.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Release Lifecycle (the spine); Stages 6-7 Failure model; Stage 1 Bump selection; CLI Surface & Flags.

title: Phase 2: AI Release Notes Engine, Change Map & Interactive Review
AI transport layer (content-agnostic)
description:
Problem: Notes generation separates two concerns. AI transport is the content-agnostic half: given an assembled prompt + ai_command, run the call, validate (non-empty/not-error/not-refusal), retry once on bad content, return the body. Knows nothing about git/diffs/tags/Change Map. Without it the engine can't invoke the AI in a trivially testable, isolated way.

Solution: A transport package taking a composed prompt + ai_command (default 'claude -p'), piping the prompt to the command's stdin via the Phase 1 CommandRunner stdin affordance, reading the body from stdout under ~60s timeout, validating sanity, retrying exactly once on bad CONTENT, returning the body or a typed transport failure. A timeout is NOT retried.

Solution note: content-agnostic half only. Do NOT build diff assembly, exclusion, max_diff_lines, Change Map, or the default prompt here (tasks 2-2..2-5). Transport receives a finished prompt and returns a body or failure.

Outcome: Valid stdout body returned unchanged. Empty/whitespace/error/refusal body -> exactly one retry; second bad -> distinguishable notes failure (consumed by on_notes_failure 2-7). >~60s -> timeout failure, no retry. ai_command overridable (default 'claude -p'), split into name+args, run via CommandRunner with prompt on stdin.

Do:
- Package internal/ai (or internal/notes/transport) with a Transport holding the CommandRunner and config (ai_command, timeout).
- Generate(ctx, prompt) (string, error): parse ai_command into name+args (default claude -p -> claude, [-p]; whitespace split sufficient, operator-controlled config). Invoke via CommandRunner stdin path (RunWith from 1-1), prompt on stdin, body from stdout. ~60s timeout via context.WithTimeout.
- Validation (sanity not structure): bad if empty, whitespace-only, or looks like error/refusal (non-zero exit, or error/refusal sentinel body); no machine wrapper to validate.
- Retry: bad-content -> retry exactly once; second still bad -> typed notes-failure. Timeout / command-not-found / missing tool NOT retried -> immediate failure. Single retry bounds worst-case latency at one ~60s timeout.
- Return typed distinguishable causes (timeout, missing-tool, empty/error/refusal-after-retry) for 2-7.
- All invocation via CommandRunner; tests use FakeRunner to script stdout/exit/timeout.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Valid body returned unchanged.
- Empty/whitespace body -> one retry then notes-failure if still bad.
- Error/refusal body -> one retry then notes-failure if still bad.
- Timeout NOT retried -> distinguishable timeout failure.
- Missing AI tool (command-not-found) -> distinguishable failure, not retried.
- Prompt delivered on stdin; body read from stdout.
- ai_command overridable (default claude -p); custom command split+invoked correctly.
- Transport contains no git/diff/Change Map logic.
- All AI invocation via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: returns valid body unchanged; retries once on empty/whitespace then fails; retries once on error/refusal then fails; succeeds on second attempt when first bad; does not retry timeout; does not retry missing tool; pipes prompt to stdin reads stdout; honours overridden ai_command.

Edge Cases: empty/whitespace->retry then fail; error/refusal->retry then fail; timeout->not retried; ai_command override.

Context: Engine layering — AI transport content-agnostic: takes assembled prompt+content+ai_command, runs the call, validates, retries once, returns the body; knows nothing about git/diffs/tags; trivially testable. Engine: default claude -p, pipes prompt to stdin, reads stdout, ~60s timeout; overridable via ai_command. Validation is sanity not structure; one automatic retry; timeout not retried, straight to on_notes_failure; single retry covers empty/error/refusal content only. on_notes_failure routing is 2-7.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Engine layering / Engine / Output format & validation.

title: Diff context assembly (last_tag..HEAD, CHANGELOG.md always-excluded)
description:
Problem: The notes engine generates from the release diff and nothing else — commit messages/history deliberately not ingested. The git-aware context assembly must produce the last_tag..HEAD diff with the built-in always-exclude of CHANGELOG.md via git :(exclude) pathspec, so the AI never sees mint's changelog churn. Foundational input the max_diff_lines guard, Change Map, and prompt build on.

Solution: A context-assembly function running git diff {last_tag}..HEAD through CommandRunner with the built-in CHANGELOG.md exclusion as a git :(exclude) pathspec, returning the post-exclusion diff text. Exclusion is path-based (git filters), never commit-based.

Solution note: Phase 2 uses ONLY the built-in always-exclude of CHANGELOG.md. Configurable diff_exclude globs and strategy-aware version_file exclusion are Phase 3 — do NOT implement here. Diff base is last_tag..HEAD (prior-tag releases); first-release never reaches this path (fixed-body precedence winner 2-10).

Outcome: Given prior tag and HEAD, returns last_tag..HEAD diff with CHANGELOG.md changes excluded. A diff where only CHANGELOG.md changed returns empty (feeds degenerate path 2-8). A gitignored-but-force-added (tracked) file still appears (not special-cased). Exclusion via git pathspec, not post-filtering text.

Do:
- internal/notes (assembly side, distinct from 2-1 transport): AssembleDiff(ctx, lastTag) (string, error).
- Build diff via CommandRunner: git diff {lastTag}..HEAD -- . ':(exclude)CHANGELOG.md'. Let git perform exclusion; do not strip CHANGELOG hunks in Go.
- CHANGELOG.md is the repo-root changelog; non-configurable built-in exclusion, excluded in both forward and regenerate paths.
- Diff base last_tag..HEAD: previous-release tag (current latest, 1-3) to HEAD. HEAD is post-hook HEAD in later phases; Phase 2 has no hooks so HEAD is working HEAD.
- Exclusion is path-based never commit-based: a git range diff operates on paths/content and cannot subtract commits.
- Force-added gitignored files: tracked, can appear in commit-to-commit diff; deliberate, not special-cased.
- Return raw post-exclusion diff text (Change Map preamble and max_diff_lines capping layered by other tasks).

Acceptance Criteria:
- Diff computed for last_tag..HEAD via CommandRunner.
- CHANGELOG.md excluded via git :(exclude) (git filters, not Go text stripping).
- Change set whose only modification is CHANGELOG.md -> empty post-exclusion diff.
- Force-added gitignored (tracked) file still appears.
- Exclusion path-based — no attempt to drop commits.
- Configurable diff_exclude and version_file exclusion NOT present (Phase 3).
- All git calls via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: diffs last_tag..HEAD; excludes CHANGELOG.md via exclude pathspec; CHANGELOG.md-only diff empty after exclusion; force-added gitignored tracked file still appears; returns post-exclusion diff text for downstream layering.

Edge Cases: CHANGELOG.md-only changes excluded -> empty diff; force-added gitignored still tracked -> appears; no source change after exclude -> empty diff.

Context: Source of truth — mint generates from the release diff and nothing else; commit messages/history not ingested. Diff base last_tag..HEAD. Diff exclusion via git :(exclude); built-in always-exclude CHANGELOG.md (non-configurable), excluded in forward and regenerate paths. Release diff is commit-to-commit, only tracked files; gitignored never appear; force-added gitignored is tracked so can appear — deliberate, not special-cased. Exclusion path-based never commit-based. diff_exclude globs and strategy-aware version_file exclusion are Phase 3.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Source of truth / Diff base / Diff exclusion.

title: max_diff_lines guard (default 50000)
description:
Problem: A huge diff is slow, costly, and summarises to mush — a cost+quality problem, not a context-window problem. mint needs a guard at the large end: if the post-exclusion diff exceeds max_diff_lines (default 50000), generation is a notes failure routed through on_notes_failure, rather than sending a giant diff. Lines are a cheap token proxy. Excluded paths must not count.

Solution: A line-count guard layered onto context assembly: count lines of the POST-exclusion diff (output of 2-2), compare to max_diff_lines (default 50000, configurable), signal a distinguishable 'diff too large' notes failure when exceeded so the caller routes it through on_notes_failure (2-7).

Solution note: too-big member of the don't-run-AI-on-bad-sized-diff family; too-small/empty member is degenerate stub (2-8). Trimmed-diff escalation lever explicitly parked (not built in Phase 2).

Outcome: post-exclusion diff of exactly 50000 lines passes; 50001 is a notes failure. Limit overridable via config. Lines from excluded paths never count (count runs on already-excluded diff).

Do:
- Extend context-assembly side (internal/notes) with a guard on the post-exclusion diff text from 2-2.
- Count lines of post-exclusion diff (newline count; document whether trailing partial line counts, be consistent and tested). Already post-exclusion, so excluded paths inherently not counted.
- Compare against max_diff_lines: default 50000, read from shared top-level config key (Phase 2 reads only needed keys with defaults; full schema validation Phase 6). Boundary inclusive — exactly max passes; strictly greater fails.
- On exceed, return distinguishable 'diff too large' notes failure (cause for 2-7's message, e.g. 'diff exceeds max_diff_lines (N > 50000)'). Do NOT call the AI when over.
- Do NOT implement the parked escalation (Change Map + trimmed diff instead of failing at cap) — note as deferred in a comment.
- Wire max_diff_lines from config, default 50000 when absent.

Acceptance Criteria:
- post-exclusion diff of exactly 50000 lines passes.
- 50001 lines -> 'diff too large' notes failure, AI not called.
- max_diff_lines configurable; custom limit honoured at its boundary.
- Excluded-path lines do not count (count on post-exclusion diff only).
- too-large failure distinguishable so on_notes_failure (2-7) can route and name it.
- Trimmed-diff escalation NOT implemented (deferred).

Tests: diff of exactly max_diff_lines passes; over max is a notes failure and skips AI; custom override honoured at its boundary; excluded-path lines do not count; too-large failure carries distinguishable cause.

Edge Cases: exactly 50000 passes; 50001/over -> notes failure; configurable override; excluded paths not counted.

Context: max_diff_lines guard — default 50000, not a context limit but a cost+quality guard; lines a cheap token proxy ~10-20 tokens/line; excluded paths don't count; exceeding = notes failure -> abort-or-fallback per on_notes_failure; fully overridable. Degenerate release: one family of don't-run-AI-on-bad-sized-diff guards: too-big -> fallback/abort; too-small/empty -> stub no AI. Intermediate lever (Change Map + trimmed diff) noted for future, revisit only on observed need. max_diff_lines is a shared top-level engine key. Full fail-loud config validation Phase 6.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 max_diff_lines guard / Degenerate release / Big-diff handling.

title: Change Map salience preamble
description:
Problem: The motivating failure — glosses over the big feature on big releases — is a salience problem (misallocated attention), not missing data; more raw diff makes it worse. mint needs a computed Change Map telling the AI what to prioritize: structural novelty (new/removed/renamed paths, especially new directories/packages) weighted ABOVE raw magnitude, with directory/area rollup and individually-notable files called out. Prepended to the AI input.

Solution: A Change Map builder running cheap git commands (name-status/numstat over last_tag..HEAD, after the same CHANGELOG.md exclusion) to derive structural novelty and per-area magnitude, then render a compact preamble: novelty first (new dirs/packages, renamed/removed), magnitude as supporting context, rolled up by directory/area with notable files (new top-level entries, single largest file) called out. Computed AFTER exclusion.

Solution note: salience metadata, not content. Computed after CHANGELOG.md exclusion (2-2) so bulk noise removed and post-exclude magnitude trustworthy. Phase 2 exclusion is only CHANGELOG.md; diff_exclude/version_file arrive Phase 3 but the map already runs after whatever exclusion exists.

Outcome: For a diff introducing a new auth/ package, the map headlines that new directory ABOVE a larger-line-count refactor of an existing area. Renamed/removed paths reported as structural. Per-area churn ranked as supporting magnitude (400 here, 3 there). Single largest file and new top-level entries called out. All changes in one existing area -> rollup by that area with no spurious novelty headline. Rendered preamble suitable to prepend ahead of the diff.

Do:
- internal/notes (assembly side): BuildChangeMap(ctx, lastTag) (string, error) (or fold into assembler), cheap git via CommandRunner:
  - git diff --name-status {lastTag}..HEAD -- . ':(exclude)CHANGELOG.md' for A/M/D/R status — structural-novelty signal.
  - git diff --numstat {lastTag}..HEAD -- . ':(exclude)CHANGELOG.md' for per-file added/removed line counts — magnitude.
- Structural novelty (primary): new/removed/renamed paths, especially new directories/packages (a path under a top-level dir with no prior entries). Weight novelty above magnitude in ordering and emphasis.
- Magnitude (secondary): roll per-file churn to directory/area granularity, rank areas by churn; supporting context not headline.
- Call out individually-notable files: new top-level entries and the single largest file (by churn). Flat per-file list is itself mush; rollup is salience-preserving.
- Render compact preamble: novelty first, then magnitude rollup, then notable files. Concise metadata, not a diff restatement.
- Compute after exclusion (:(exclude)CHANGELOG.md) so the map reflects what the AI sees.
- Produces the Change Map string; prepending to the diff is wired in 2-5/2-6. Prompt discipline (rank with map, describe from diff) authored in 2-5.

Acceptance Criteria:
- New directory/package headlined as structural novelty above a larger-magnitude existing-area change.
- Renamed and removed paths reported as structural changes.
- Per-area churn ranked and presented as supporting magnitude.
- Single largest file and new top-level entries called out individually.
- All changes within one existing area -> rollup by area, no false novelty headline.
- Map computed after CHANGELOG.md exclusion (excluded churn never appears).
- Output a compact preamble suitable to prepend before the diff.
- All git calls via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: new directory/package headlines above a larger existing-area change; reports renamed and removed paths; ranks per-area churn as supporting magnitude; calls out single largest file; calls out new top-level entries; all-in-one-existing-area rolls up with no false headline; computes map after CHANGELOG.md exclusion.

Edge Cases: new directory/package headline above magnitude; renamed/removed paths; single largest file called out; all changes in one existing area (rollup, no false headline).

Context: Change Map — computed Change Map mint assembles (cheap git) and prepends to AI input. Structural novelty (primary): new/removed/renamed paths, especially new dirs/packages; a whole new auth/ package is the strongest language-agnostic headline; weighted above raw magnitude. Magnitude (secondary): per-area churn ranking as supporting context. Granularity directory/area rollup with notable files called out. Computed after diff_exclude (after exclusion, never before). Prompt discipline: rank with the Change Map but describe from the diff; metadata not content. AI input = Change Map preamble then post-diff_exclude (max_diff_lines-capped) diff, nothing else. Phase 2 only exclusion is CHANGELOG.md.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Change Map.

title: Default notes prompt & Keep-a-Changelog emoji-skin format
description:
Problem: The AI must return notes directly in presentation format (no machine labels) following mint's default format: a TL;DR one-liner over emoji-headed Keep a Changelog sections, empty sections omitted, version-number bumps ignored, no preamble/meta-commentary. mint exposes two prompt knobs — context (inject project guidance) and prompt (full override file). Without the default prompt and knobs the transport has nothing meaningful to send.

Solution: A prompt composer building the full AI input from mint's default KaC emoji-skin prompt (carrying format rules and Change-Map salience discipline), the Change Map preamble (2-4), and the post-exclusion/max_diff_lines-capped diff (2-2/2-3). Injects [release].context into the default prompt when set; fully replaces the default with a [release].prompt file when set (mint still supplies Change Map + diff).

Solution note: owns prompt composition and default prompt text. The AI call is the transport (2-1); normal-path wiring (assembly->prompt->transport->body) is 2-6. Exact emoji-category mapping and wording are ship-and-refine — sensible defaults, tunable.

Outcome: Default prompt instructs: TL;DR one-liner (may be multi-line) over emoji-headed KaC sections (Added/Changed/Deprecated/Removed/Fixed/Security, e.g. emoji Added/Changed/Fixed/Removed), omit empty sections, bold+describe notable features, unit of entry = notable item (not file/hunk/commit), ignore version bumps, no preamble/meta-commentary, rank with Change Map but describe from diff, Deprecated/Security only on explicit textual marker. Configured context injected; configured prompt file fully overrides while mint still supplies Change Map + diff.

Do:
- internal/notes: ComposePrompt(changeMap, diff, cfg) string assembling full AI input: prompt instructions + Change Map preamble + post-exclusion (capped) diff — nothing else.
- Author the default prompt text encoding all rules: TL;DR one-liner at top (unified cross-change narrative from whole diff; may be multi-line) above categorized sections; emoji-headed sections keyed to KaC taxonomy Added/Changed/Deprecated/Removed/Fixed/Security with emoji headers, omit empty sections entirely; unit of entry = notable item (a change that adds a feature and fixes a bug yields two items in two sections); notable features bolded+described; ignore version-number bumps and trivial bookkeeping churn; strict no-preamble no-meta-commentary; salience discipline rank-with-map describe-from-diff; diff-inferability tiers Added/Changed/Fixed/Removed diff-readable, Deprecated/Security opportunistic only on explicit marker (@deprecated annotation; obvious security surface auth/crypto/input-validation, CVE-referencing dep bump), light guidance, expected empty most releases.
- Two prompt knobs: [release].context (string or file) INJECT into default prompt (does not replace), common case; [release].prompt (file path) FULL OVERRIDE — replace default text with file contents, mint still appends Change Map + diff; a theme/variant is just a prompt override, no theme enum.
- AI returns presentation format directly — no machine wrapper, this task produces only input; no output parsing.
- Read only [release] keys needed (context, prompt) with defaults (absent = default prompt, no injection); full schema validation Phase 6.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Composed input = prompt + Change Map preamble + post-exclusion (capped) diff, in that order, nothing else.
- Default prompt instructs TL;DR one-liner, emoji-headed KaC sections, omit empty, bold+describe notable, notable-item unit, ignore version bumps, no preamble/meta-commentary.
- Default prompt carries salience discipline (rank with Change Map, describe from diff).
- Deprecated/Security instructed as opportunistic — only on explicit textual marker.
- [release].context (string or file) injected without replacing.
- [release].prompt file fully overrides default while mint still supplies Change Map + diff.
- No machine-parseable output wrapper requested.

Tests: composed input is prompt + change map + capped diff in order; default prompt requests TL;DR over emoji-headed KaC sections; instructs omit-empty and ignore-version-bumps; carries no-preamble/no-meta-commentary; carries rank-with-map-describe-from-diff; Deprecated/Security only on explicit marker; configured context string injected; configured prompt file fully overrides.

Edge Cases: context inject (string or file) appended not replacing; prompt full-override file used verbatim with Change Map + diff still supplied; Deprecated/Security only on explicit marker.

Context: Default notes format anchors on Keep a Changelog rendered in mint's emoji skin; TL;DR one-liner above categorized sections; emoji-headed sections keyed to KaC taxonomy Added/Changed/Deprecated/Removed/Fixed/Security; empty sections omitted; unit of entry = notable item; notable features bolded+described; diff-inferability tiers Added/Changed/Fixed/Removed readable, Deprecated/Security opportunistic on explicit marker; strict no-preamble no-meta-commentary; ignore version-number bumps. Prompt control: context injects into default prompt, prompt is full override (mint still supplies diff); a theme/variant is just a prompt override file. Output: AI returns presentation format directly, no machine wrapper. Exact emoji-category mapping and wording ship-and-refine.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Default notes format / Prompt control / Output format & validation / Change Map (prompt discipline).

title: Normal AI notes path wiring (prior-tag release)
description:
Problem: Assembly (diff, max_diff_lines, Change Map — 2-2/2-3/2-4), the prompt composer (2-5), and the transport (2-1) exist as separate pieces but nothing threads them into the single normal AI path taking a prior-tag release from last_tag to a validated notes body — the engine's core value-add for the common case.

Solution: A notes-generation entry point for the normal AI path orchestrating: assemble post-exclusion diff -> apply max_diff_lines guard -> build Change Map -> compose prompt (Change Map + capped diff + default/overridden prompt, with context injected) -> call transport -> return validated body WHOLE. No parsing, splitting, or per-sink reassembly.

Solution note: normal AI path only. Notes-path precedence (first-release/degenerate/--no-ai winning before this runs) is 2-10; on_notes_failure routing of this path's failures is 2-7. Produces the body and surfaces failures; does not decide abort-vs-fallback.

Outcome: For a prior-tag release with a real post-exclusion diff, returns the AI body exactly as generated (whole). Valid generation passes through unchanged. Transport/guard failure surfaces as a typed notes failure for the caller to route. Body is a single string suitable for all three sinks.

Do:
- internal/notes: Generate(ctx, lastTag, cfg) (string, error) for the normal AI path, orchestrating in order:
  1. AssembleDiff(lastTag) (2-2) -> post-exclusion diff.
  2. max_diff_lines guard (2-3) -> on exceed return 'diff too large' notes failure (do not call AI).
  3. BuildChangeMap(lastTag) (2-4) -> preamble.
  4. ComposePrompt(changeMap, diff, cfg) (2-5) -> full AI input (context injection / prompt override applied).
  5. transport.Generate(prompt) (2-1) -> validated body or typed notes failure (after one retry / timeout rules).
- Return the body WHOLE — no parsing/splitting/label extraction/per-sink reassembly. Same string flows to every sink (2-11).
- Surface a typed notes failure (cause: too-large, timeout, empty/error/refusal-after-retry, missing tool); do NOT decide abort vs fallback (that is on_notes_failure 2-7).
- Degenerate-diff short-circuit (empty/all-excluded -> stub, no AI) is 2-8, sits in front via precedence (2-10); this task assumes a non-degenerate diff when invoked (document the assumption).

Acceptance Criteria:
- For a prior-tag release with a real diff, returns a validated AI body.
- Body returned WHOLE — no parsing/splitting/reassembly.
- Valid generation passes through unchanged.
- Pieces invoked in order: assemble diff -> max_diff_lines guard -> Change Map -> compose prompt -> transport.
- A guard or transport failure surfaces as a typed notes failure (cause preserved) without deciding abort/fallback.
- AI input is exactly Change Map + capped diff + prompt (no commit messages).
- All external calls via CommandRunner/FakeRunner.

Tests: returns validated AI body for a prior-tag release with a real diff; uses body whole with no parsing/splitting; valid generation passes through unchanged; invokes assemble->guard->change map->compose->transport in order; too-large diff surfaces notes failure without calling AI; transport failure surfaces typed notes failure with cause preserved.

Edge Cases: body used whole (no parsing); valid generation passes through unchanged.

Context: Stage 4 — generate a notes body from the diff since the last release; same body reused for every surface; generate once use everywhere. Output: body whole for every sink, no parsing/splitting/per-sink reassembly. AI input = Change Map preamble then post-diff_exclude (max_diff_lines-capped) diff, nothing else. Engine layering separates assembly (git-aware) from transport (content-agnostic). Notes-path precedence and on_notes_failure routing are 2-10 and 2-7.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 intro / Engine layering / Change Map / Output format & validation.

title: on_notes_failure resolution (abort default / fallback)
description:
Problem: When the normal AI path can't produce a body (missing tool, timeout, error, diff exceeds max_diff_lines, or bad/empty generation surviving one retry), mint must decide what to do — fail loud by default. Notes generate before the tag, so aborting leaves nothing tagged; an empty/garbage release is worse than a failed command. on_notes_failure (default abort, opt-in fallback) governs ONLY the normal AI path.

Solution: An on_notes_failure resolver taking a normal-path notes failure (from 2-6) and the config setting, either aborting loudly (default, tagging nothing) or proceeding with a non-AI fallback body (commit-subject list since last tag, or a fixed configurable string). Governs only the normal path — first-release/degenerate/--no-ai never reach it.

Outcome: on_notes_failure=abort (default): any normal-path failure aborts loudly before the tag, message naming the cause (timeout, diff too large, etc.). on_notes_failure=fallback: same failure proceeds with the fallback body — default commit-subject list since last tag, or a fixed configurable string if set. Varied causes route the same way.

Do:
- internal/notes (or small resolver alongside orchestrator): ResolveFailure(failure, cfg) (body string, abort error):
  - Read on_notes_failure from [release] config; default abort.
  - abort (default): return abort error naming the failure cause; run stops before the tag (nothing tagged/pushed). Presenter surfaces it (wired in 2-16).
  - fallback: return a fallback body, no abort. Default = commit-subject list since last tag (git log --format=%s {last_tag}..HEAD via CommandRunner); if a fixed fallback string configured, use that.
- Build commit-subject-list fallback body via CommandRunner (metadata used only as a fallback record — NOT the AI input; AI never ingests commit messages).
- Scope strictly to the normal AI path: invoked only when 2-6 surfaces a failure. first-release/degenerate/--no-ai never invoke it; ensure precedence resolver (2-10) does not route them here.
- Share fallback-body construction with the --no-ai path (2-9) — factor a fallback-body builder both consume.

Acceptance Criteria:
- on_notes_failure defaults to abort: normal-path failure aborts before the tag with cause named, tagging nothing.
- on_notes_failure=fallback: normal-path failure proceeds with the fallback body.
- Fallback body defaults to the commit-subject list since last tag.
- A configured fixed fallback string used instead when set.
- Varied causes (timeout, missing tool, empty-after-retry, diff too large) all route through the same resolution.
- Resolver governs only the normal AI path (never for first-release/degenerate/--no-ai).
- Commit-subject list built via CommandRunner/FakeRunner, never fed to the AI.

Tests: abort (default) aborts before the tag and names the cause; fallback proceeds with the commit-subject list body; fallback uses a fixed configurable string when set; timeout/missing tool/empty-after-retry/too-large all route through resolution; resolver not invoked for first-release/degenerate/--no-ai.

Edge Cases: abort default -> tags nothing; fallback -> commit-subject list; fallback -> fixed configurable string; varied failure causes.

Context: Failure behaviour — notes generate at Stage 4 before the tag so aborting leaves nothing tagged; on_notes_failure default abort (missing tool/timeout/error/diff exceeds max_diff_lines/bad-empty after one retry) -> fail loud, tag nothing; empty/garbage release worse than a failed command. fallback mode (opt-in) -> proceed with a non-AI body; fallback defaults to commit-subject list since last tag, can be a fixed configurable string. on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path; steps 1-3 never call the AI. Commit messages never the AI input — commit-subject list only a fallback record.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Failure behaviour / Notes-path precedence.

title: Degenerate-diff stub path
description:
Problem: An empty or all-excluded diff is the one input the AI will reliably hallucinate on. A re-tag with no source change, a release where every changed file fell under exclusion, or pure churn with nothing notable must NOT be sent to the AI. mint needs the guard at the small end: if the post-exclusion diff is empty or whitespace-only, write a minimal honest stub entry and never call the AI.

Solution: A degenerate-diff detector inspecting the post-exclusion diff (2-2): if empty or whitespace-only, produce a minimal honest stub body (version header context + a short stub line, e.g. 'Maintenance release — no notable source changes') and short-circuit — AI never invoked, no hard error, no skipped entry.

Solution note: too-small/empty member of the don't-run-AI-on-bad-sized-diff family (too-big is max_diff_lines 2-3). Precedence step 2 (above --no-ai, below first-release); precedence wiring is 2-10. Phase 2 all-excluded means only CHANGELOG.md exclusion; diff_exclude arrives Phase 3 but this detector already runs on whatever post-exclusion diff exists.

Outcome: When the post-exclusion diff is empty (only CHANGELOG.md changed), all-excluded, or whitespace-only, mint writes a minimal stub entry (version header + short honest stub line) with NO claude invocation recorded. A real release still produces a truthful record; no hallucination, no hard error.

Do:
- internal/notes: IsDegenerate(diff string) bool (empty or whitespace-only post-exclusion diff) and StubBody() returning the minimal honest stub line (e.g. 'Maintenance release — no notable source changes'); wording tunable in code, ship a sensible default.
- Detector consumes the post-exclusion diff from 2-2, so all-changed-files-excluded and only-CHANGELOG.md-changed both reduce to an empty post-exclusion diff — handle uniformly.
- On degenerate, short-circuit: do NOT call the transport (2-1), do NOT build a Change Map for the AI. The stub body flows to the sinks like any other body (2-11), under the version header in the changelog (## [x.y.z] - date from Phase 1's writer).
- Whitespace-only diffs count as degenerate (pure churn with nothing notable).
- This path offers only y/n/e at the review gate (no r, no AI to nudge) — gate variant is 2-14; this task just produces the stub body and the no-AI signal.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Empty post-exclusion diff -> stub body with no AI invocation.
- All-excluded diff (every changed file excluded; Phase 2 = only CHANGELOG.md changed) treated as degenerate.
- Whitespace-only diff treated as degenerate.
- Stub is a minimal honest entry (version header + short stub line), not a hard error and not a skipped entry.
- AI/claude never invoked on the degenerate path.
- Stub body is a normal body flowing to the sinks (2-11).

Tests: empty post-exclusion diff produces a stub with no AI call; all-excluded diff is degenerate (only CHANGELOG.md changed); whitespace-only diff is degenerate; stub is a minimal honest entry not an error or skipped entry; AI never invoked on the degenerate path.

Edge Cases: all files fell under exclusion; whitespace-only diff; no notable source change; AI never invoked.

Context: Degenerate release — if the post-diff_exclude diff is empty or whitespace-only (re-tag with no source change; every changed file fell under diff_exclude; or pure churn with nothing notable), mint does not call the AI; writes a minimal honest entry: version header + a short stub line (e.g. Maintenance release — no notable source changes); no hallucination, no hard error, no skipped entry. One family of don't-run-AI-on-bad-sized-diff guards: too-big -> fallback/abort; too-small/empty -> stub no AI. Precedence (2): degenerate diff -> stub entry no AI. Phase 2 only exclusion is CHANGELOG.md.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Degenerate release / Notes-path precedence.

title: Deliberate skip: --no-ai fallback path
description:
--no-ai deliberate-skip path: bypasses the AI entirely and produces the fallback body (commit-subject list since last tag, or a fixed configurable string), and NEVER aborts even when the AI would have failed; tag never empty.

Solution: reuses the same fallback-body builder as on_notes_failure=fallback (2-7). Precedence step 3 (below first-release and degenerate, above the normal AI path); precedence wiring is 2-10. on_notes_failure does not govern it; the review gate omits r (2-14).

Do:
- internal/notes --no-ai body provider: commit-subject list since last tag (git log --format=%s {last_tag}..HEAD via CommandRunner), or a fixed configurable string when set.
- Reuse fallback-body builder shared with 2-7.
- Never abort. If last-tag log empty, still yield non-empty body (fixed string / minimal record); coordinate with degenerate stub (precedence places it above --no-ai).
- Wire --no-ai CLI flag to select this path (behaviour here; end-to-end 2-16, precedence 2-10). Gate offers only y/n/e (no r) — variant 2-14.

Acceptance Criteria:
- --no-ai produces commit-subject list body, no AI invocation.
- fixed configurable fallback string used instead when set.
- never aborts even when normal AI path would have failed.
- tag body never empty.
- fallback-body builder shared with on_notes_failure=fallback (2-7).
- on_notes_failure does not govern --no-ai.

Tests: produces commit-subject list body no AI call; uses fixed configurable fallback string when set; never aborts even when AI would have failed; yields non-empty body when no commits since last tag; shares fallback-body builder with on_notes_failure.

Edge Cases: never aborts even when AI would fail; commit-subject list body; fixed-string fallback config.

Context: --no-ai is a deliberate skip not a failure -> always fallback body, never aborts. Precedence (3): --no-ai -> fallback body (commit-subject list), never aborts. Optionality stack: --no-ai / no AI -> tag body falls back to commit-subject/changed-files list, tag never empty. Fallback body defaults to commit-subject list since last tag, can be a fixed configurable string — shared with on_notes_failure=fallback.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Failure behaviour / Notes-path precedence; Body Distribution Optionality stack.

title: Notes-path precedence resolution
description:
Problem: Multiple notes guards can apply to a single run, and which body mint uses depends on strict precedence. mint must resolve in order: (1) first-release fixed body, (2) degenerate stub, (3) --no-ai fallback, (4) normal AI path. Getting it wrong means e.g. --no-ai triggering the AI, or a first-release run routed through on_notes_failure. This resolver is the single decision point selecting which path produces the body.

Solution: A precedence resolver that, given run state (prior tag present?, --no-ai?, post-exclusion diff) and config, selects exactly one notes path in fixed order: first-release > degenerate > --no-ai > normal AI. Routes each run to the right body provider (1-9, 2-8, 2-9, 2-6) and ensures on_notes_failure (2-7) governs only the normal AI path.

Outcome: first-release (no prior tag) always uses 'Initial release.' and wins over --no-ai and a degenerate diff — no AI, no on_notes_failure. Degenerate wins over --no-ai (stub, no AI). --no-ai wins over normal AI (fallback, no AI, never aborts). Only when none of the first three apply does the normal AI path run, failures routed through on_notes_failure.

Do:
- internal/notes: SelectBody(ctx, state, cfg) (body string, path Kind, err error) resolving precedence strictly:
  1. First release (no prior tag) -> fixed body 'Initial release.' (1-9), no AI. Wins over everything below — no diff base, so --no-ai, on_notes_failure, and the degenerate check do not apply.
  2. Degenerate diff (empty / all-excluded post-exclusion diff — 2-8) -> stub, no AI.
  3. --no-ai (deliberate skip — 2-9) -> fallback body, never aborts.
  4. Normal AI path (2-6) -> generate + validate; on failure route to on_notes_failure (2-7).
- Evaluate first-release BEFORE consulting --no-ai or degenerate; degenerate BEFORE --no-ai. For first-release the degenerate check must not even run (no diff base).
- Ensure on_notes_failure consulted ONLY in branch 4. Branches 1-3 never call the AI / never route through on_notes_failure.
- Return which path was taken (a Kind) so the review gate (2-14) knows whether to offer r (only on normal AI path) and the wiring (2-16) can report it.
- Single decision point invoked by end-to-end wiring (2-16); composes existing body providers, does not reimplement them.

Acceptance Criteria:
- First-release (no prior tag) selects 'Initial release.' and wins over --no-ai and a degenerate diff.
- First-release never runs the degenerate check or on_notes_failure (no diff base).
- A degenerate diff wins over --no-ai (stub, no AI).
- --no-ai wins over the normal AI path (fallback, no AI, never aborts).
- The normal AI path runs only when none of the first three apply.
- on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path (branches 1-3 never reach it).
- Resolver reports which path was taken (for gate r-omission and reporting).

Tests: first-release wins over --no-ai and a degenerate diff; first-release does not run the degenerate check or on_notes_failure; a degenerate diff wins over --no-ai; --no-ai wins over the normal AI path; the normal AI path runs only when no earlier guard applies; on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path; resolver reports the selected path kind.

Edge Cases: first-release wins over --no-ai and degenerate; degenerate wins over --no-ai; on_notes_failure only governs the normal path.

Context: Notes-path precedence — resolves in order: 1 first release (no prior tag) -> fixed body 'Initial release.' no AI, wins over everything below (no diff base, so --no-ai, on_notes_failure, degenerate check don't apply); 2 degenerate diff (empty/all-excluded post-diff_exclude) -> stub entry no AI; 3 --no-ai (deliberate skip) -> fallback body (commit-subject list) never aborts; 4 normal AI path -> generate+validate, failures route to on_notes_failure. on_notes_failure governs only the normal AI path; steps 1-3 never call the AI.

Spec Reference: specification.md — Stage 4 Notes-path precedence.

title: Single-body distribution to all sinks
description:
